User.title: User
User.description: Represents a user of the ReelRecs application, storing their profile information.

id.description: Unique identifier for the User entity.

username.description: A unique username chosen by the user for identification.

email.description: The user's email address, used for account management.

createdAt.description: Timestamp when the user account was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp when the user account was last updated.

Movie.title: Movie
Movie.description: Represents a movie with its core details, used for recommendations and display.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Movie entity.

title.description: The official title of the movie.

synopsis.description: A brief summary or description of the movie's plot.

director.description: The name of the movie's primary director.

actors.description: A list of the main actors or key cast members in the movie.

posterUrl.description: URL to the movie's poster image.

genres.description: A list of genres associated with the movie, useful for filtering and recommendations.

releaseDate.description: The theatrical release date of the movie.

UserWatchedMovie.title: UserWatchedMovie
UserWatchedMovie.description: Represents a record of a user having watched a specific movie, linking users to their watched movie history. (Relationship: User N:N Movie)

id.description: Unique identifier for the UserWatchedMovie entity.

userId.description: Reference to the User who watched the movie. (Relationship: User 1:N UserWatchedMovie)

movieId.description: Reference to the Movie that was watched. (Relationship: Movie 1:N UserWatchedMovie)

watchedAt.description: Timestamp when the user marked the movie as watched.

definition.description: Stores user profile information, accessible only by the owning user. This path structure ensures authorization independence by directly using the authenticated user's UID.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier (UID) of the user, matching `request.auth.uid`.

definition.description: Contains global, publicly accessible details for each movie. This collection has a homogeneous security posture, allowing broad read access for recommendations.

params.name: movieId
params.description: The unique identifier for the movie.

definition.description: Records movies that a specific user has marked as watched. This subcollection ensures that each user's watched list is private and directly owned by them, leveraging the `{userId}` in the path for authorization independence. Each document includes the `userId` and `movieId` for data integrity.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier (UID) of the user who watched the movie, matching `request.auth.uid`.

params.name: watchedMovieId
params.description: The unique identifier for this specific watched movie record within the user's list (can be an auto-generated ID or a movieId if suitable).

firestore.reasoning:
The recommended Firestore structure for ReelRecs prioritizes Authorization Independence and Structural Segregation to ensure robust, debuggable security rules and efficient data access. The core principle applied is path-based ownership for user-specific data and global collections for public entities. User profiles are stored in a top-level collection `/users/{userId}`, where `{userId}` directly corresponds to the authenticated user's UID. This setup makes authorization trivial, as rules can directly compare `request.auth.uid` with the `{userId}` in the path, eliminating any need for `get()` calls to check parent document ownership. Movie data, which is global and publicly accessible, resides in a separate top-level collection `/movies/{movieId}`. This segregation ensures that all documents within `/movies` share the same public read security posture, simplifying rules.

For tracking watched movies, the `UserWatchedMovie` entity is implemented as a subcollection under each user's profile: `/users/{userId}/watchedMovies/{watchedMovieId}`. This design is crucial for Authorization Independence, as the `userId` is part of the path, allowing rules to authorize `read` and `write` access by comparing `request.auth.uid` with the path's `userId`. This makes operations atomic and prevents security rule dependencies on parent document attributes. Each `UserWatchedMovie` document also contains `userId` and `movieId` for data integrity and easier querying if needed, but its security relies fundamentally on the path.

Regarding QAPs (Rules are not Filters), this structure enables secure list operations. For `/movies`, a `list` query simply retrieves publicly available movie data, fulfilling the security requirements without server-side filtering. For `/users/{userId}/watchedMovies`, a `list` query made by an authenticated user to their own `watchedMovies` subcollection (`/users/request.auth.uid/watchedMovies`) will inherently return only their own watched movies, as the rules enforce that the `userId` in the path matches `request.auth.uid`. This design ensures that security rules explicitly define what data can be accessed, and `list` operations naturally adhere to these boundaries, rather than relying on rules to filter results after retrieval.
